perturbation.original_text: 1.1 "Maximum Daily Delivery Obligation (MDDO)" means the maximum daily quantity of natural gas, expressed in Dekatherms (Dth), that Transporter is obligated to deliver from time to time at the Point(s) of Delivery specified in Exhibit B to the executed Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 "Maximum Daily Delivery Obligation (MDDO)" means the typical daily quantity of natural gas, expressed in Dekatherms (Dth), that Transporter intends to deliver, but is not obligated to, at the Point(s) of Delivery specified in Exhibit B to the executed Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the 'Maximum Daily Delivery Obligation' from a strict obligation to a non-binding intention. This introduces ambiguity about the transporter's commitment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 CFR § 284.13 - Standards of conduct and reporting requirements for interstate pipelines.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 CFR § 284.13 mandates that interstate pipelines operate in a fair and non-discriminatory manner. By changing the MDDO to a non-binding intention, the transporter could potentially discriminate against shippers, as there is no longer a guaranteed delivery obligation. This undermines the principle of fair access to pipeline transportation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 CFR § 284.13 - Reporting requirements for interstate pipelines. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 18 CFR § 284.13 - Reporting requirements for interstate pipelines. CFR prev | next § 284.13 Reporting requirements for interstate pipelines. An interstate pipeline that provides transportation service under subparts B or G of this part must comply with the following reporting requirements. (a) Cross references. The pipeline must comply with the requirements in Part 358, Part 250, and Part 260 of this chapter, where applicable. (b) Reports on firm and interruptible services. An interstate pipeline must post the following information on its Internet web site, and provide the information in downloadable file formats, in conformity with § 284.12 of this part, and must maintain access to that information for a period not less than 90 days from the date of posting. (1) For pipeline firm service and for release transactions under § 284.8 , the pipeline must post with respect to each contract, or revision of a contract for service, the following information no later than the first nomination under a transaction: (i) The full legal name of the shipper, and identification number, of the shipper receiving service under the contract, and the full legal name, and identification number, of the releasing shipper if a capacity release is involved or an indication that the pipeline is the seller of transportation capacity; (ii) The contract number for the shipper receiving service under the contract, and, in addition, for released transactions, the contract number of the releasing shipper's contract; (iii) The rate charged under each contract; (iv) The maximum rate, and for capacity release transactions not subject to a maximum rate, the maximum rate that would be applicable to a comparable sale of pipeline services; (v) The duration of the contract; (vi) The receipt and delivery points and t
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the 'Maximum Daily Delivery Obligation (MDDO)' from a strict obligation to a non-binding intention. The Law Explanation suggests that this could undermine fair access to pipeline transportation under 18 CFR § 284.13. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 focuses on reporting requirements, the Law Explanation uses the same CFR section to argue that removing the guaranteed delivery obligation could lead to discrimination. By making the MDDO a non-binding intention, the transporter could potentially discriminate against shippers, as there is no longer a guaranteed delivery obligation. This deviation from a 'maximum daily quantity' that the 'Transporter is obligated to deliver' to a 'typical daily quantity' that the transporter 'intends to deliver, but is not obligated to' directly contradicts the concept of a guaranteed service, which is related to fair and non-discriminatory practices.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2 Transportation service rendered hereunder shall be firm/interruptible service as described in Section 2 of Transporter's X Rate Schedule FTS _____ Rate Schedule ITS.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2 Transportation service rendered hereunder shall be 'flexible' service with elements of both firm and interruptible service, at the Transporter's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces the defined service types (firm/interruptible) with 'flexible' service, removing the defined categories and giving the transporter complete control over whether a service is firm or interruptible, departing from traditional regulatory standards.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 CFR § 284.8 - Rates for natural gas transportation.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 CFR § 284.8 addresses rates for natural gas transportation, particularly concerning firm and interruptible services. Introducing 'flexible' service without defining its rate structure or service parameters circumvents the established rate structures and potentially leads to unjust or unreasonable rates, contravening the regulatory oversight of transportation rates.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 CFR § 284.8 - Release of firm capacity on interstate pipelines. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 18 CFR § 284.8 - Release of firm capacity on interstate pipelines. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 284.8 Release of firm capacity on interstate pipelines. (a) An interstate pipeline that offers transportation service on a firm basis under subpart B or G of this part must include in its tariff a mechanism for firm shippers to release firm capacity to the pipeline for resale by the pipeline on a firm basis under this section. (b) (1) Firm shippers must be permitted to release their capacity, in whole or in part, on a permanent or short-term basis, without restriction on the terms or conditions of the release. A firm shipper may arrange for a replacement shipper to obtain its released capacity from the pipeline. A replacement shipper is any shipper that obtains released capacity. (2) The rate charged the replacement shipper for a release of capacity may not exceed the applicable maximum rate, except that no rate limitation applies to the release of capacity for a period of one year or less if the release is to take effect on or before one year from the date on which the pipeline is notified of the release. Payments or other consideration exchanged between the releasing and replacement shippers in a release to an asset manager as defined in paragraph (h)(3) of this section are not subject to the maximum rate. (c) Except as provided in paragraph (h) of this section, a firm shipper that wants to release any or all of its firm capacity must notify the pipeline of the terms and conditions under which the shipper will release its capacity. The firm shipper must also notify the pipeline of any replacement shipper designated to obtain the released capacity under the terms and conditions specified by the firm shipper. (d) The pipeline must provide notice of offers to release or
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the defined firm/interruptible service with 'flexible' service at the transporter's discretion. The Law Explanation states that 18 CFR § 284.8 concerns rates for natural gas transportation, particularly regarding firm and interruptible services. The introduction of 'flexible' service without defining its rate structure undermines the established rate structures, which potentially violates regulatory oversight. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that pipelines must include in their tariff a mechanism for firm shippers to release firm capacity and mentions firm shippers are permitted to release their capacity, highlighting the importance of defined firm service parameters. This deviation from standard categories creates a legal contradiction, as the contract now fails to align with regulatory definitions and expectations for transportation service types.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 Shipper shall pay Transporter for the service hereunder an amount determined in accordance with Transporter's FTS/ITS Rate Schedule, and the General Terms and Conditions of Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff, all as may be revised from time to time. Such FTS/ITS Rate Schedule and General Terms and Conditions are incorporated by reference and made a part hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 Shipper shall pay Transporter for the service hereunder an amount determined by Transporter based on prevailing market conditions and internal cost assessments. While the Transporter aims to align with the FTS/ITS Rate Schedule as a benchmark, the final amount is at the Transporter's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This modification allows the Transporter to deviate from the established FERC-approved rate schedules (FTS/ITS), introducing uncertainty and potential for arbitrary pricing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 717c - Rates and charges; schedules; suspension of new rates.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S. Code § 717c requires that natural gas companies file rate schedules with the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) and that these rates be just and reasonable. Granting the Transporter the discretion to set rates based on market conditions and internal cost assessments, independent of the FERC-approved schedules, directly violates this requirement for regulated and transparent rate-making.

Rates and charges (a) Just and reasonable rates and charges All rates and charges made, demanded, or received by any natural-gas company for or in connection with the transportation or sale of natural gas subject to the jurisdiction of the Commission, and all rules and regulations affecting or pertaining to such rates or charges, shall be just and reasonable, and any such rate or charge that is not just and reasonable is declared to be unlawful. (b) Undue preferences and unreasonab
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 15 U.S. Code § 717c(a), which mandates that all rates and charges by natural gas companies for transportation or sale of natural gas subject to the Commission's jurisdiction must be just and reasonable. The original text adheres to this by referencing the Transporter's FERC-approved rate schedule. The changed text, however, allows the Transporter to set rates based on 'prevailing market conditions and internal cost assessments,' explicitly deviating from the FERC-approved schedules. This violates the requirement for just and reasonable rates as determined by FERC, as stated in the scraped snippet: 'All rates and charges made, demanded, or received by any natural-gas company...shall be just and reasonable, and any such rate or charge that is not just and reasonable is declared to be unlawful.'
